tasks.name: N3: DPHY Tx integration
tasks.description:
LDO Results: [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/p/shovan/IQDUsZno00LvRLHfwsEzRjeZAWTD\_dS6G7hp0sWhK-xLSrs?e=hmmSHs](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/p/shovan/IQDUsZno00LvRLHfwsEzRjeZAWTD_dS6G7hp0sWhK-xLSrs?e=hmmSHs)
This is the LDO Result and PM>60 for i\_Load <5mA. And it's giving 500mV as LDO\_Output. For Reference Please check from Slide No 14 Onwards.
D PHY Tx Integrated result(LDO+8:1bit Serializer+Pre-Driver+Driver) : [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/p/shovan/IQDrqTDWPzmjTZoUDNKHmytoAcMo3IET9gl2tyV-l10lydQ?e=lb4O5b](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/p/shovan/IQDrqTDWPzmjTZoUDNKHmytoAcMo3IET9gl2tyV-l10lydQ?e=lb4O5b)
This Result tells How The Approach has been Done for D PHY Tx

**_Summary:_**
In D PHY Tx, We are Using a LDO for 500mV Vreg and there is a 8:1 bit Serializer which Works in the 1GHz But Frequency and There after we are Using Pre-Driver and Two Differential Driver where We need 50 ohm terminated resistance which is Achieved by the tuning 32 Unit Cells (By Default 24 are Turned ON to get 50 ohm Terminated Resistance)

tasks.name: N3: QS222 Understanding and Python codebase review
tasks.description:
Why? Before silicon debugging, getting familiarized with 222 architectures will help in urgent issues debugging while testing.
b. Deliverable: A ppt representing the understanding of 222
c. Risk: None
d. Limitations: Lack of experience with PSK and ADC systems might require extra ramp up.
e. Detailed Description: The 222 architecture contains the following main features: Adiabatic Tx, QAM 2-6 support, DSSS. The Tx and Rx architecture for each of these features is mostly similar with some changes here and there. Start with the QAM architecture and go over the following blocks and use the QS222 KT lecture series to understand: Blocks at Tx: Pulse shaping, and half-band interpolation, NCO, DAC. Blocks at Rx: Analog FE, ADC, half-band filter, RRC MF, pkt detector, Analog AGC, TED, interpolator, PED, CFO and de-rotation. Also, see how adiabatic Tx is implemented and what changes at Tx and Rx for DSSS spreading. Also, go over how ADR is implemented. f. Link: QS222\_understanding.pptx
[https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/shovan\_10xar\_com/Documents/Tapeout/QS222/Spec/QS222\_understanding.pptx?d=wac34546fd70e45bead61558693389aa3&csf=1&web=1&e=Iubtv7](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/shovan_10xar_com/Documents/Tapeout/QS222/Spec/QS222_understanding.pptx?d=wac34546fd70e45bead61558693389aa3&csf=1&web=1&e=Iubtv7)

tasks.name: N3: DVCO verification
tasks.description:
Why? Changing the originally proposed DVCO calibration algorithm and making the required changes in the algorithm so that RTL and py algo match. Deliverable: Change the original py algo to match with RTL and meetings with verification team to sync up and discuss. Risk: None Limitation: Sometimes it might not be possible to every digital aspect in py. Description: a) Explain both the initial calibration algo and continuous code monitoring to verification team. B) Since the DVCO count depends on initial phase, change the condition of search to take the count inaccuracy. C) Measure the count @freq using the RTL data not the floor(T\_g\*freq) relation. D) Explain how to add frequency drifts for ccm. E) Condition of storing code in LUT: abs(curr-ref)-abs(prev-ref)>a, a=0,1,2,3. There can be maximum count inaccuracy of 3 so we have to allow a to be 3. This may cause us to vary from optimal fine code, but we are within +/-10M of desired frequency, given that we don’t allow gate time<0.5us.
F) Check the condition: If curr>=ref: Store curr\_code. This condition eliminates the need to modify the search condition for different count errors.
G) Keep an option to change ccm code by +2,-2 as well and analyze whether ccm will be able to correct frequency drifts for worst case. QS223\_digital\_algos.pptx
tasks.creator: KiritkumarP

tasks.name: N3: CDR for rippled ED O/P
tasks.description:
Why: Need to know how to handle IF close to f\_d either by CDR or by better pulse design because in our case IF is very close to f\_d
Deliverable: Ppt comparing various CDR and methods to shapes the pulse better
Risk: Not doing this task can cause suboptimal BER performance
Limitations: Theoretically I-MM cdr performance better than osr cdr, but I-MM CDR suffers from some locking issue in silicon which can’t be replicated in py.
Description: Due to IF being very close to F\_d, the ED\_LPF can’t filter out all the ripples present in its input. Due to this, the CDR is expected to perform worse:
Possible solutions: better pulse design or smarter CDR -
For better pulse design we can either increase IF which increases FE power or use a better filter for ED which is expected to give only little benefit because IF is very close is f\_d, fundamentally some ripple will always remain.
For smarter CDR:
Quantify by how much the performance degrades as opposed to ideal pulse.
Use the I-MM CDR and osr-CDR and see how much their performance degrades.
Look for methods to enhance the performance for these two CDR.
Look for other potential CDR.
Compare the performance of these CDR with baud-rate osr CDR.
Compare the effect of changing the phase of the pulse
Compare the effect of changing IF.
Further, couple the effect of CDR and ED\_LPF B/W to enhance the performance
Result link: [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/shovan\_10xar\_com/\_layouts/15/doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4762976c-df8a-49aa-b845-701d1e1344e5%7D&file=fhss\_qs223\_python\_sims.pptx&action=edit&mobileredirect=true](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/shovan_10xar_com/_layouts/15/doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4762976c-df8a-49aa-b845-701d1e1344e5%7D&file=fhss_qs223_python_sims.pptx&action=edit&mobileredirect=true) Slide 55-69

tasks.name: N3: Effect of AC coupling Zero IF receiver & Acceptable HPF cutoff
tasks.description:
Why : AC coupling systems helps remove effects of DC offsets, for relatively high DR cases the AC coupling doesnt hurt a lot, only if a long strings of 1s or 0s come in a Zero IF system then it starts to degrade performance 

Deliverable : 
1) If AC coupling can work for the current IQ Zero IF arch
2) If yes then what HPF filter cut-off to take (settling time needs to be taken in account & maximum run length to be supported)

Risk
1) Assume some maximum run length (typically 5 or 7), anything higher might need whitening 
2) Settling time for HPF might be modelled inaccurately, affects wake up time & duty cycling power efficiency
Result:
1) Python and mathematical model assuming ED to be simply 'X^2' (X = input across it), leads to AC coupling before ED after mixer to be infeasible.
2) Discussions with Aniruddh C and Sumon : ED model maybe inaccurate, need to update it and check in Synopsys sim
tasks.creator: KiritkumarP

tasks.name: N3: Ramp up - Compare homodyne & hetrodyne Rx arch performance
tasks.description:
Why : Heterodyne arch
          1) uses single 50% duty cycled mixer (easier to design clocking, less power)
          2) can have AC coupled system (upto ED), easier offset correction 
          3) I-Q matching not required, easier layout & matching considerations 
          4) Uses >2x Data B/W for TIA, LPF & ED, but uses only single path 
          5) Easier to match impedance from antenna 
          6) Initial theory predicts 3db poorer sensitivity, Subodh's results would prove/disprove the theory 
          7) Requires Tx & Rx DCO to have well defined Freq offset (need finer freq steps than Fd)  
8) Requires image reject filters (high side or low side depending on sign of freq offset used), Might be prohibitively expensive in a FHSS system, but we might be able to get away without it (Needs discussion)

Limitations: Image rejection problem for heterodyne arch needs deeper discussion 

 Homodyne arch
          1) uses two 25% duty cycled mixer (difficult to design clocking, more power)
          2) DC coupled system, offset correction needs to be checked
          3) I-Q matching required, layout & matching considerations 
          4) Uses ~1x Data B/W for TIA, LPF & ED, but uses two paths 
          5) Needs complex impedance matching, harder than matching real impedance (using single mixer) 
          6) Requires Tx & Rx DCO to have low Freq offset (Step size = Fd) 

Deliverable 
1) BER vs Attenuation plots for 2 arch at 0 offset (best case)
2) BER vs Attenuation plots 3 arch across phase & freq offset errors 

Limitation 
Modelling error might be present, might need a review 

Risk
Slight delay can be expect as Subodh is still new
tasks.creator: KiritkumarP

comments.text:
Kirit: Delay reason 
1) While designing the UWB filter, we had to do a couple of iterations to meet the bandwidth requirements. 
2) We changed the LPF layout a few times to reduce parasitics onthe  LPF o/p node. We had to bring RSSI and AGC i/p close to the LPF output to reduce the parasitic cap. 
3) UWB Clock buffer: We changed the clock buffer layout a few times to meet the delay requirements. 
4) We had to modify the integrator to meet the CDR PLL 320MHz requirements 
5) There was a change in BR_mixer and BR LPF design 
6) Added source follower at the output of ED and LPF 
7) Changed floor plan many times to meet the top-level routing requirements and optimize the critical signal path. (We brought BE_top closer to the ED o/p and SS_top closer to the LPF/ED o/p. 
8) There was a change in the Spectrum sensor design.
comments.author: KiritkumarP

Limitation: Avalability of Silico, stability of Silicon set up and proper config for checking new mode
Analog non-linearity of ED
Need custom settings for 127S FE and BE settings, might cause delays

tasks.name: N3: ED bandwidth (settling time)
tasks.description:
Why: Need spec for EDs analog implementation.
Deliverables: Bandwidth and selling time required for ED
Risk: ED arch may have to change
Limitation: Improper ED modeling can lead to improper spec and other non-linearity may affect overall performance
Result: ED bandwidth to be 100MHz using heterodyne architecture. FD=40M, IF=40M , so min ED BW=80MHz. So 100MHz with margin.
